## Introduction

Propositional logic is the study of the inferential relations that obtain
between compound propositions, such as:

$$
\textrm{Alice is a philosophy major \emph{and} Bob is majoring in economics.}
$$

$$
\textrm{\emph{Either} the train is late \emph{or} the schedule that is posted online is wrong}.
$$

$$
\textrm{\emph{If} the battery is not dead, \emph{then} the computer will turn on.}
$$

$$
\textrm{You are eligible to vote \emph{if and only if} you are a resident of the county}.
$$

Such compound propositions are called 'truth-functional' compounds, or, 'Boolean'
compounds, for short.[^Boole] Truth-functional propositional logic forms the
core of most other systems of logic utilized in philosophical analysis,
including first-order quantificational logic and modal logic.[^classical] It
also provides the standard context in which the theory of probability is
developed. It thus constitutes the first essential entry in a philosophy
student's logical toolkit. In this chapter, we will introduce a formal language in which to represent
arguments of propositional logic, a semantic framework in which to assess their
validity, and a proof system in which to carry out propositional reasoning.

[^Boole]:
    The label 'Boolean' is in honor of the English mathematician George
    Boole, whose seminal work, _An Investigation of the Laws of Thought_
    [-@Boole1854-ur], layed the foundation for the branch of mathematics now
    called Boolean algebra.

[^classical]:
    Truth-functional, or Boolean, propositional logic is often
    referred to as _classical_ propositional logic. This label is a misnomer,
    for truth-functional propositional logic is neither classical in the sense
    of being the first system of propositional logic to have been studied, nor
    in the sense of having its origin in Greek or Latin antiquity. The use of
    the label 'classical' to describe truth-functional propositional logic seems
    to have gained widespread usage in the mid-20th century, mainly to
    distinguish truth-functional propositional logic from alternative
    non-classical systems of propositional logic (such as intuitionistic
    propositional logic or relevantist propositional logic) that began to be
    studied more widely at that time.
